title: Asukas name order
wikitext:
[]: Asuka's name order             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Mon Aug 27, 2012 6:56 pm Could someone explain me what's up with Asuka's name order in the English media/fandom? Asuka's name in NGE is 惣流・アスカ・ラングレー while in NME her name is 式波・アスカ・ラングレー, they only changed her Japanese surname, yet quite counter-intuitively we have Asuka Langley Soryu in NGE and Asuka Shikinami Langley in NME. So, ahem, what is the correct westernisation of Asuka's name?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Aug 27, 2012 8:39 pm The Roman print equivalent of 惣流・アスカ・ラングレー in Japan has always been Soryu Asuka Langley. This obviously isn't a proper way of rendering her name for Anglosphere purposes -- and is downright nonsensical when it's appearing right alongside Western Name Order versions of the other cast -- -- so translators and fans corrected it by putting the "Soryu" on the end, hence "Asuka Langley Soryu". As for Shikinami, it has been rendered in the order "Asuka Shikinami Langley" by khara's own J-to-E translator Dan Kanemitsu. He similarly opted for "Mari Makinami Illustrious". (I think the former has only appeared in the CRC book, while the latter showed up in the actual movie.) It's not what we expected, but it's official, so...

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Mon Aug 27, 2012 9:31 pm Reichu wrote: so translators and fans corrected it by putting the "Soryu" on the end, hence "Asuka Langley Soryu".

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Mon Aug 27, 2012 9:34 pm ^ ^ I see, thanks for your reply. Reichu wrote: It's not what we expected, but it's official, so...

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Mon Aug 27, 2012 10:22 pm Double surnames aren't common here in the U.S., but they are used. You'll usually see them rendered with a hypen, though...at least, in my experience. Asuka'd be Asuka Langley-Soryu.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Mon Aug 27, 2012 10:30 pm ^ Thanks, just out of curiosity, is it possible for a child to take both parents' surnames? Wikipedia only mentions the case of women taking husbands' surnames. Anyway an hyphenated surname would have solved Asuka's awkward half Japanese, half western name order, it goes without saying that they probably didn't even consider if they're used or not in the States and they only wanted to accentuate the hafu effect...

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Mon Aug 27, 2012 10:37 pm Hyper Shinchan wrote: ^ Thanks, just out of curiosity, is it possible for a child to take both parents' surnames? Wikipedia only mentions the case of women taking husbands' surnames. Anyway an hyphenated surname would have solved Asuka's awkward half Japanese, half western name order, it goes without saying that they probably didn't even consider if they're used or not in the States and they only wanted to accentuate the hafu effect...

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Mon Aug 27, 2012 10:50 pm riffraff11235 wrote: As a matter of fact, it is. I have a friend - who coincidentally is Japanese - who signs with both his parents' surnames.

[]: Hyphenated Names... so easily confused. B/             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Tue Aug 28, 2012 12:05 am Hyper Shinchan wrote: ^ Thanks, just out of curiosity, is it possible for a child to take both parents' surnames?

[]:             Quote Post by AR-99 » Tue Aug 28, 2012 11:05 am In some instances where the parents aren't married the child may use both parents' surnames in a hyphenated version, as mentioned above. Married women may also retain their maiden name along with the husband's surname, again in a hyphenated version.

[]:             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Tue Aug 28, 2012 6:21 pm Asuka/Shinji shippers would have a nightmare naming their kids if they were writing a fanfic... "OK, should I name her Yuko Langley Soryu Ikari or Yuko Ikari Langley Soryu?!"

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Tue Aug 28, 2012 6:25 pm driftking18594 wrote: "OK, should I name her Yuko Langley Soryu Ikari or Yuko Ikari Langley Soryu?!"

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Aug 28, 2012 7:33 pm Reichu wrote: The Roman print equivalent of 惣流・アスカ・ラングレー in Japan has always been Soryu Asuka Langley. This obviously isn't a proper way of rendering her name for Anglosphere purposes -- and is downright nonsensical when it's appearing right alongside Western Name Order versions of the other cast -- -- so translators and fans corrected it by putting the "Soryu" on the end, hence "Asuka Langley Soryu". As for Shikinami, it has been rendered in the order "Asuka Shikinami Langley" by khara's own J-to-E translator Dan Kanemitsu. He similarly opted for "Mari Makinami Illustrious". (I think the former has only appeared in the CRC book, while the latter showed up in the actual movie.) It's not what we expected, but it's official, so...

[]:             Quote Post by Justacrazyguy » Tue Aug 28, 2012 7:43 pm Bagheera wrote: As for hyphenated names: in Latino cultures the father's first surname is typically first, while the mother's first surname is typically second.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Aug 28, 2012 7:45 pm Justacrazyguy wrote: Are you sure? Here in Portugal the father´s last surname comes last and the mother´s last surname comes in first.

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Tue Aug 28, 2012 8:18 pm Bagheera wrote: He can render it how he likes, but as I recall the actual names for each are Shikinami Asuka Langley and Makinami Mari Illustrious

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Tue Aug 28, 2012 8:26 pm JoeD80 wrote: The Q-preview at the end of 2.0 uses Mari Makinami Illustrious.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Aug 28, 2012 8:33 pm JoeD80 wrote: The Q-preview at the end of 2.0 uses Mari Makinami Illustrious.

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Tue Aug 28, 2012 8:52 pm Wasn't talking about how it should be done. It's the official translation, so that's what we're stuck with.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Aug 28, 2012 9:06 pm JoeD80 wrote: Wasn't talking about how it should be done. It's the official translation, so that's what we're stuck with.
